title: Gynecomastia from Androgen Deficiency or Resistance: A Case-Based Atlas (10 Published Cases)
subtitle: A resident training module across four etiologic buckets — primary/secondary hypogonadism, hyperprolactinemia, congenital & acquired androgen resistance, and drugs that block androgen action; with the gonadotropin-led work-up algorithm and a board-style question bank.
authors: Resident training module — synthesis of 10 published case reports (see sourceFile); original teaching text
sourceFile: Synthesis of indexed case reports/series in JCEM Case Reports, Pan African Medical Journal, Annales d'Endocrinologie, Clinical Endocrinology, Psychiatric Services, J Clin Res Pediatr Endocrinol, The Lancet/Gut, Int J Appl Basic Med Res, Andrologia, and Pediatrics/Ann Intern Med (PMIDs/DOIs cited in prose).

relatedChapters.title: Pubertal Gynecomastia That Is Not Physiological

patient.chiefComplaint: Gynecomastia driven by androgen deficiency or androgen resistance — a rise in the estrogen-to-androgen ratio at the breast
pmh: Secondary hypogonadism (Kallmann syndrome; macroprolactinoma; drug-induced hyperprolactinemia)
Androgen resistance (congenital PAIS; acquired in untreated celiac disease)

caseDescription: A case-based atlas of pathologic gynecomastia organised by the mechanism that lowers androgen tone at the breast. Ten published cases span four buckets — (A) androgen deficiency from primary or secondary hypogonadism, (B) hyperprolactinemia that produces androgen deficiency indirectly, (C) androgen-resistance disorders (congenital and acquired), and (D) drugs that interfere with androgen action. However different the upstream cause, all ten converge on one final common pathway: unopposed estrogen action on glandular breast tissue once the estrogen-to-androgen ratio rises. The diagnostic art is reading the testosterone level together with the gonadotropins (and prolactin, DHT, and a drug history) to place a given patient in the right bucket.
keyFindings: Final common pathway: ↑ estrogen-to-androgen ratio at the breast, from ↓ androgen production OR ↓ androgen-receptor signalling
Low T + high LH/FSH = primary hypogonadism; low T + low/normal LH = secondary; high T + high LH = androgen resistance
An inappropriately normal LH in a man with very low testosterone obligates a pituitary MRI (superimposed secondary cause)
Most drug-induced gynecomastia occurs with normal serum testosterone — the lesion is androgen-receptor blockade at the breast
Acquired androgen resistance of untreated celiac disease (high T, high LH, low DHT) mimics 5α-reductase deficiency but is reversible
finalDiagnosis: Gynecomastia is a sign, not a diagnosis: management is entirely cause-specific. Replace testosterone in established hypogonadism (and image the pituitary when gonadotropins are inappropriate); suppress prolactin with a dopamine agonist for prolactinoma and de-prescribe or switch the offending drug for drug-induced hyperprolactinemia; recognise androgen resistance biochemically (high T + high LH) and treat the reversible causes (strict gluten-free diet in celiac disease); and for anti-androgen drugs, dechallenge to a non-anti-androgenic alternative (eplerenone for spironolactone, famotidine for cimetidine, stopping the 5α-reductase inhibitor) before considering tamoxifen or surgery for fibrotic, burnt-out tissue.
learningObjectives: Use the testosterone/LH/FSH pattern (plus prolactin, DHT and a drug history) to place gynecomastia into the correct etiologic bucket.
Explain why primary hypogonadism and androgen resistance both raise the estrogen-to-androgen ratio despite very different testosterone levels.
Recognise the 'inappropriately normal gonadotropin' clue that unmasks a superimposed secondary (pituitary) cause.
List the drugs that cause gynecomastia by androgen-receptor antagonism at normal serum testosterone, and their preferred substitutes.
Distinguish congenital androgen insensitivity from 5α-reductase deficiency and from the reversible acquired resistance of untreated celiac disease.

items.title: Why androgen deficiency and androgen resistance both end in gynecomastia
items.content: Gynecomastia is benign proliferation of glandular breast tissue in a male, and pathologic gynecomastia rooted in androgen deficiency or androgen resistance is a critical differential because the missed diagnoses (Klinefelter syndrome, Kallmann syndrome, prolactinoma, partial androgen insensitivity, drug effects) carry consequences for fertility, bone, psychology and even cancer surveillance. However different the upstream lesion, every cause in this atlas converges on the same final common pathway: a rise in the estrogen-to-androgen ratio acting on breast tissue. That ratio can rise because androgen production falls (hypogonadism), because the androgen receptor cannot read a normal or high testosterone signal (resistance), or because a drug blocks the receptor or shunts testosterone toward estradiol. Pubertal/physiologic gynecomastia is the common benign mimic to exclude; the pathologic causes are where the gonadotropins, prolactin, DHT and the drug history earn their keep.
keyPoints: Common final pathway: unopposed estrogen action once the estrogen:androgen ratio rises
Three routes there: ↓ androgen production, defective AR signalling, or drug interference
Pathologic gynecomastia threatens fertility, bone, psychosocial health and (in KS) breast-cancer risk

items.title: How to sort the causes into four etiologic buckets
items.content: This atlas mirrors a four-bucket taxonomy. Bucket A — androgen deficiency from primary or secondary hypogonadism: low testosterone lowers androgen tone at the breast (Klinefelter, Kallmann, amiodarone-induced testicular toxicity). Bucket B — hyperprolactinemia causing androgen deficiency: high prolactin suppresses GnRH → low LH/FSH → low testosterone (macroprolactinoma, antipsychotic-induced). Bucket C — androgen-resistance disorders: testosterone is normal or high but receptor signalling (or T→DHT conversion) is defective (congenital PAIS; acquired resistance in untreated celiac disease). Bucket D — drugs that interfere with androgen action: AR blockade, 5α-reductase inhibition, or a shifted estrogen/testosterone ratio, usually with normal serum testosterone (spironolactone, finasteride, cimetidine). The single most useful sorting step is reading testosterone together with LH/FSH: low T + high gonadotropins = primary; low T + low/normal gonadotropins = secondary; high T + high LH = resistance.
keyPoints: A: hypogonadism (low T) — primary (↑LH/FSH) vs secondary (↓/normal LH)
B: hyperprolactinemia — indirect androgen deficiency via GnRH suppression
C: androgen resistance — normal/high T with high LH
D: anti-androgen drugs — gynecomastia at normal serum T

items.title: How testosterone, DHT, estradiol, prolactin and SHBG set breast-tissue androgen tone
items.content: Testosterone is converted to the more potent androgen dihydrotestosterone (DHT) by 5α-reductase, and to estradiol by aromatase (CYP19A1). The androgen receptor (AR) is a nuclear transcription factor encoded on Xq11–12; loss of DHT, AR blockade, or AR mutation all reduce the androgenic signal a breast cell receives. LH drives Leydig-cell testosterone production while FSH drives Sertoli-cell function and spermatogenesis; in primary testicular failure both rise, and Sertoli-cell inhibin-B selectively restrains FSH. Prolactin suppresses GnRH (via kisspeptin neurons) and directly inhibits Leydig-cell steroidogenesis, so hyperprolactinemia causes a secondary hypogonadism. SHBG binds testosterone with higher affinity than estradiol, so anything that changes SHBG shifts the free-hormone balance. Read together, these explain every pattern in the atlas: e.g. high LH stimulating residual Leydig-cell aromatase raises intratesticular estradiol in primary hypogonadism, while a 5α-reductase inhibitor leaves testosterone available for aromatization to estradiol.
keyPoints: T → DHT (5α-reductase, potent androgen) and T → E2 (aromatase/CYP19A1)
Inhibin-B from Sertoli cells selectively suppresses FSH → FSH can be normal when LH is high
Prolactin lowers androgens by suppressing GnRH and Leydig-cell steroidogenesis
SHBG binds T > E2, so SHBG shifts free-hormone ratios

items.title: Why an inappropriately normal LH in a man with very low testosterone demands a pituitary MRI — Klinefelter with superimposed macroadenoma (Case 1)
items.content: A 37-year-old man with lifelong low libido and a sense of never fully completing puberty was tall (193 cm), obese, with sparse pubertal hair, Tanner-3 gynecomastia and small firm testes (<4 mL). Testosterone was 43 ng/dL (300–1080) with free T 8.1 pg/mL, FSH was high at 28.3 mIU/mL — but LH was only 10.8 mIU/mL, inappropriately normal for such profound hypogonadism. Karyotype was 47,XXY and a pituitary MRI (ordered because of that LH discrepancy) found an 8×7×14 mm non-functioning macroadenoma (Mani et al., JCEM Case Reports 2024, ENDO 2024 abstract 6597; PMC11453621). Klinefelter causes primary testicular failure — seminiferous tubule hyalinization and Leydig-cell dysfunction — so both gonadotropins should be elevated; the non-elevated LH unmasked a superimposed secondary hypogonadism from the adenoma. Testosterone replacement normalised T and improved libido and gynecomastia; the non-functioning adenoma was followed with serial MRI. The teaching rule: karyotype every man with small testes + high FSH + low T, and when a gonadotropin is inappropriately low for the testosterone, image the pituitary.
keyPoints: Klinefelter is profoundly underdiagnosed (~25% identified in life) — karyotype small testes + ↑FSH + ↓T
Inappropriate-gonadotropin rule: a non-elevated LH in primary hypogonadism → hunt a superimposed secondary cause (MRI, prolactin, iron studies)
Breast-cancer risk in KS is ~10–20× that of 46,XY men — annual breast exam
Testosterone restores virilization but not fertility; micro-TESE+ICSI retrieves sperm in ~40–50%

items.title: Why you must ask every boy with absent puberty about his sense of smell — Kallmann syndrome (Case 2)
items.content: A 17-year-old presented with failure to develop secondary sexual characteristics, eunuchoid proportions (arm span 5 cm > height), Tanner-1 hair, micropenis (2.5 cm), barely palpable gonads and year-long gynecomastia — and had no sense of smell, which he had never recognised as abnormal. There was a maternal history of anosmia and childhood cryptorchidism repair. Testosterone was 0.42 ng/mL with LH <0.3 and FSH 0.5 mIU/mL (hypogonadotropic), karyotype 46,XY, and MRI showed bilateral olfactory-bulb agenesis with a 4 mm non-functioning microadenoma (Ben Salem et al., Pan Afr Med J 2018;29:222; DOI 10.11604/pamj.2018.29.222.12808; PMC5911661). Kallmann syndrome is failed embryonic migration of GnRH neurons from the olfactory placode (e.g. KAL1/anosmin-1), so there is no pulsatile GnRH → no LH/FSH → secondary hypogonadism, with concurrent olfactory-bulb agenesis producing anosmia. Gynecomastia arises because residual adrenal androgens are aromatized peripherally while androgen-mediated suppression of breast tissue is absent during the pubertal window. Testosterone induced virilization and regressed the gynecomastia; fertility can later be induced with hCG±FSH or pulsatile GnRH. The rule: anosmia is the Rosetta Stone of congenital hypogonadotropic hypogonadism — ask, because the patient won't volunteer it.
keyPoints: Anosmia + absent puberty + low LH/FSH = Kallmann syndrome
MRI shows olfactory-bulb agenesis/hypoplasia in ~75%; a normal MRI doesn't exclude it
Micropenis + cryptorchidism in infancy should prompt an endocrine work-up
Fertility is achievable with hCG+FSH or pulsatile GnRH even after years of T replacement

items.title: Why primary hypogonadism causes gynecomastia more readily than secondary — amiodarone testicular toxicity (Case 3)
items.content: A 57-year-old on amiodarone 200 mg/day for 6 years developed 8 months of tender bilateral gynecomastia then loss of libido and erectile dysfunction; six months earlier he had had two self-limited episodes of acute testicular pain. Testes had shrunk to ~6 mL (from a documented 18 mL) and he had slate-grey amiodarone facial pigmentation. Testosterone was 148 ng/dL with LH 32.5 and FSH 41.8 mIU/mL (hypergonadotropic), normal prolactin and thyroid function, and ultrasound showed bilateral atrophy with heterogeneous echotexture (Gohier et al., Ann Endocrinol 2024;85(5):445; PMC11542941). Amiodarone accumulates in testicular tissue and causes a chemical orchiepididymitis; recurrent subclinical inflammation damages tubules and Leydig cells, lowering testosterone and releasing feedback so LH and FSH rise. Critically, that high LH stimulates aromatase in the residual Leydig cells, raising the intratesticular and systemic estradiol-to-testosterone ratio — which is why primary hypogonadism (high LH) drives gynecomastia more readily than secondary hypogonadism (where T and E2 fall together). Stopping amiodarone (switched to sotalol) and replacing testosterone resolved the tenderness and halved the gynecomastia. Recovery from drug-induced testicular toxicity is variable and may be incomplete.
keyPoints: Primary > secondary for gynecomastia: high LH drives residual-Leydig-cell aromatase → higher E2:T
Amiodarone endocrine toxicity is broader than thyroid — testicular toxicity is underrecognised
Testicular pain preceding gynecomastia points to a testicular (primary) cause
Other acquired primary causes: mumps orchitis, trauma/torsion, radiation, alkylating chemotherapy

items.title: How a prolactinoma causes gynecomastia without ever touching the breast directly — male macroprolactinoma (Case 4)
items.content: A 44-year-old man had 6 months of tender bilateral gynecomastia, loss of libido and erectile dysfunction, reduced shaving frequency and a year of bitemporal headache. He had expressible milky discharge (galactorrhea, present in only ~8% of hyperprolactinemic men) and formal perimetry confirmed a bitemporal superior quadrantanopia. Prolactin was ~18,400 mIU/L (≈870 ng/mL), testosterone 5.2 nmol/L (≈150 ng/dL) with low-normal LH/FSH, and MRI showed a 2.3 cm sellar/suprasellar mass compressing the optic chiasm (representative presentation aligned with the cohort of Pinzone et al., Clin Endocrinol 1997;47:677–684; PMID 9448878). Mechanistically the tumour autonomously secretes prolactin, which suppresses kisspeptin/GnRH so LH and FSH are inappropriately low for the degree of hypogonadism — a secondary hypogonadism; the low testosterone, not any direct prolactin action on the breast, produces the gynecomastia. Cabergoline shrank the tumour, normalised prolactin and testosterone, decompressed the chiasm and regressed the gynecomastia. Male prolactinomas are usually macroadenomas at diagnosis (~70%) because the symptoms are insidious.
keyPoints: Prolactin causes gynecomastia indirectly — by creating an androgen-deficient (secondary hypogonadal) state
Male prolactinomas present late and large; galactorrhea is rare (male breast lacks estrogen/progesterone priming)
Cabergoline is first-line and usually normalises testosterone — separate T replacement rarely needed
Screen mild/moderate elevations with PEG precipitation to exclude macroprolactinemia

items.title: Why risperidone is the antipsychotic most likely to cause gynecomastia and galactorrhea — drug-induced hyperprolactinemia (Case 5)
items.content: A 36-year-old man with bipolar disorder and treated primary hypothyroidism started risperidone 2.5 mg twice daily and on day 12 developed bilateral milky galactorrhea and breast enlargement. Prolactin was 48.2 ng/mL with normal testosterone, LH, FSH and estradiol; no pituitary imaging was done because the tight temporal link and modest elevation pointed to a drug cause (Madhusoodanan et al., Psychiatr Serv 2000;51(8):983–985; DOI 10.1176/appi.ps.51.8.983; PMID 10913444). Risperidone (and paliperidone) are among the most prolactin-elevating antipsychotics because of strong dopamine-D2 blockade, which removes tonic dopaminergic inhibition of lactotrophs. Here testosterone stayed normal, so prolactin acting on a primed breast — amplified by the patient's hypothyroidism, where TRH acts as a prolactin-releasing factor — best explains the galactorrhea (a 'double hit'). Notably the symptoms resolved spontaneously within 3 weeks with only a dose reduction, illustrating lactotroph tolerance. The management hierarchy for antipsychotic hyperprolactinemia is: reduce dose → switch to a prolactin-sparing agent (aripiprazole, quetiapine, clozapine) → add adjunctive aripiprazole (partial D2 agonist) → low-dose cabergoline as a last resort (small risk of psychosis exacerbation). Galactorrhea in a man warrants prolactinoma exclusion, but a drug cause is the commonest overall.
keyPoints: Risperidone/paliperidone are the most prolactin-elevating atypicals; aripiprazole is prolactin-sparing
Hypothyroidism potentiates the rise (TRH is a prolactin-releasing factor)
Stepwise management: dose reduction → switch → adjunctive aripiprazole → cabergoline
Transient hyperprolactinemia with early tolerance can occur — a short observation may suffice for mild symptoms

items.title: Why testosterone and LH are both high in partial androgen insensitivity — PAIS from a novel AR mutation (Case 6)
items.content: A 16-year-old with 2 years of progressive Tanner-4 gynecomastia and sparse facial hair had penoscrotal hypospadias, a bifid scrotum and small (≈8 mL) testes, plus a maternal-line history of adolescent breast enlargement (an X-linked pattern). Testosterone was high at 985 ng/dL with elevated LH (14.2), normal FSH, mildly raised estradiol (68 pg/mL), a normal DHT and a normal T:DHT ratio; karyotype was 46,XY and AR sequencing found a hemizygous c.1727C>T (p.Thr576Ile) mutation in exon 3 (Koçyiğit et al., J Clin Res Pediatr Endocrinol 2016;8(2):232–235; DOI 10.4274/jcrpe.2637; PMC5096480). The mutant androgen receptor reads testosterone and DHT poorly, so the hypothalamus and pituitary — also androgen-resistant — fail to feel negative feedback and drive LH up, which in turn raises testicular testosterone and provides extra substrate for aromatization to estradiol; FSH stays normal because Sertoli-cell inhibin-B (an androgen-independent signal) is intact. The result is unopposed estrogen action at a breast whose AR cannot respond, hence gynecomastia. Management was mastectomy and supraphysiologic testosterone (partial benefit), with bone-density and breast surveillance. The signature: 46,XY + high/normal T with high LH + undervirilized phenotype + pubertal gynecomastia.
keyPoints: PAIS triad: 46,XY + high/normal T with high LH + undervirilized male with pubertal gynecomastia
FSH stays normal because Sertoli-cell inhibin-B feedback is androgen-independent
Normal T:DHT ratio separates PAIS from 5α-reductase deficiency (markedly elevated ratio)
AR mutations are found in only ~30% of clinically diagnosed PAIS — coactivator/other defects exist

items.title: Why untreated celiac disease mimics 5α-reductase deficiency — reversible acquired androgen resistance (Case 7)
items.content: A 32-year-old man with celiac disease and presumed gluten-free adherence had persistent gynecomastia, low libido, muscle wasting and a 'feminized' habitus; careful history revealed ongoing covert gluten exposure (cross-contaminated oats, shared surfaces), and he had dermatitis herpetiformis. Testosterone and free T were high, yet DHT was low (18 ng/dL; 30–85), LH was elevated (18.5), FSH normal, anti-tTG IgA >200 U/mL and biopsy showed Marsh 3c villous atrophy (Green et al., Lancet 1977;309:280–282; and Farthing et al., Gut 1983;24:608–614; PMID 6682819). Untreated celiac disease produces a reversible acquired androgen resistance: villous atrophy reduces intestinal 5α-reductase activity (lowering DHT), zinc malabsorption impairs AR function, and the inflammatory cytokine milieu (TNF-α, IL-6) blunts androgen signalling — giving the paradoxical biochemical signature of high T + high LH + low DHT that looks like 5α-reductase deficiency but is acquired and reversible. A strictly enforced gluten-free diet healed the mucosa, normalised DHT and LH, brought testosterone down toward normal and resolved the gynecomastia over 12 months. The rule: always re-interrogate gluten exposure in unexplained androgen resistance — patients often believe they are compliant when they are not.
keyPoints: Paradoxical signature: high T + high LH + low DHT, mimicking 5α-reductase deficiency
Mechanisms: ↓ intestinal 5α-reductase, zinc malabsorption, inflammatory cytokines impairing AR signalling
Reversible — strict gluten-free diet normalises the axis over months
Confirm active disease (anti-tTG IgA, duodenal biopsy) before attributing gynecomastia to celiac

items.title: How spironolactone blocks the androgen receptor at the breast despite normal serum testosterone — drug-induced gynecomastia (Case 8)
items.content: A 52-year-old hypertensive man, switched from hydrochlorothiazide to spironolactone 25 mg/day for thiazide-induced hypokalemia, developed a painful firm 3 cm subareolar lump in the right breast alone 12 months later, with a completely normal left breast. Testosterone, free T, LH, FSH, estradiol and prolactin were all normal, and fine-needle aspiration showed benign ductal cells (Bhagavathula et al., Int J Appl Basic Med Res 2018;8(1):54–56; PMC5846220; PMID 29552540). Spironolactone causes gynecomastia by several synergistic mechanisms despite normal serum androgens: it antagonises the androgen receptor at target tissues including breast, displaces androgens from SHBG (raising free estradiol relative to free testosterone), increases peripheral T→E2 conversion, and at higher doses inhibits testosterone biosynthesis (CYP17A1) — the net effect is a functional androgen-deficient state at the breast. Switching to eplerenone (a selective mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist with 100–1000-fold lower AR affinity) resolved the swelling within 3 months while keeping blood pressure controlled. Unilateral presentation does not exclude a drug cause; rechallenge is unethical when a safe alternative exists.
keyPoints: Drug-induced gynecomastia typically occurs at normal serum testosterone — the lesion is AR blockade at the breast
Spironolactone gynecomastia affects 10–52% of men; risk rises with ≥50 mg/day and >6 months
Unilateral gynecomastia does not exclude a drug cause
Eplerenone is the preferred substitute (negligible AR affinity)

items.title: Why a 5α-reductase inhibitor shunts testosterone into estradiol — finasteride-induced gynecomastia (Case 9)
items.content: A 21-year-old on finasteride 1 mg/day for androgenetic alopecia (and stable levothyroxine for hypothyroidism) developed tender bilateral gynecomastia 4 months in. Testosterone and LH/FSH were normal but DHT was low (16 ng/dL; 30–85) and the T:E2 ratio had fallen below 15 (Traish et al., Andrologia 2010;42(5):327–330; PMC2929552; PMID 20860633). Finasteride is a type-II 5α-reductase inhibitor: blocking T→DHT removes the more potent androgen (DHT binds AR ~5× more avidly than testosterone), lowering total androgenic tone, while the unconverted testosterone becomes extra substrate for aromatization to estradiol — so the androgen-to-estrogen balance at the breast falls even though serum testosterone is normal. Stopping finasteride halved the gynecomastia by 3 months, but at 10 months residual fibrotic, hormonally unresponsive tissue persisted ('burnt-out' gynecomastia) and surgery was offered. Dutasteride (dual type I/II inhibitor) carries a higher risk through more profound DHT suppression. The practical message: counsel men taking a 5α-reductase inhibitor for cosmetic indications about breast changes and the possibility of persistent effects.
keyPoints: Mechanism: ↓DHT removes potent androgen and leaves T for aromatization to E2 → ↓ androgen:estrogen ratio
Finasteride 1 mg gynecomastia ~0.4–1.0%; dose-dependent and higher at the 5 mg BPH dose
Dutasteride (dual inhibitor) carries higher risk than finasteride
Resolution can be incomplete once tissue becomes fibrotic ('burnt-out') — surgery may be needed

items.title: Why cimetidine, alone among the H₂ blockers, causes gynecomastia — direct AR antagonism (Case 10)
items.content: A 45-year-old man with duodenal ulcer disease, switched by formulary from ranitidine to cimetidine 800 mg/day, developed tender bilateral subareolar gynecomastia after about 5 months, with entirely normal testosterone, LH, FSH, estradiol and prolactin (Lilly et al., Pediatrics 1980;66(2):296; PMID 7402816; supplemented by Jensen et al., Ann Intern Med 1983;99(1):51–53). Cimetidine is a direct androgen-receptor antagonist: its imidazole ring sterically occupies the AR ligand-binding pocket, competing with testosterone and DHT, and it can also mildly raise prolactin through reduced dopaminergic tone — producing functional androgen deficiency at the breast despite normal circulating androgens. Switching to famotidine (a thiazole-ring H₂ blocker with negligible AR affinity) resolved the gynecomastia within 4 months while keeping the ulcer in remission. This case crystallises the Bucket D principle that all four drug cases shared: gynecomastia from AR antagonism at the breast, not from a low serum testosterone. Cimetidine is now rarely used (superseded by PPIs and safer H₂ blockers) but remains the classic teaching example of receptor cross-reactivity.
keyPoints: Cimetidine is the only H₂ blocker with clinically meaningful anti-androgen activity (imidazole ring binds AR)
Famotidine/nizatidine (thiazole ring) and ranitidine have negligible AR affinity
Like the whole bucket, gynecomastia occurred at normal serum testosterone
Dechallenge to famotidine resolves it; switch rather than treat

items.title: How to work up gynecomastia from the testosterone-plus-gonadotropin pattern
items.content:
After history, examination and a drug review, the discriminating panel is testosterone (total + free), LH, FSH, estradiol, prolactin, TSH, β-hCG and LFTs (± renal function). Then read testosterone alongside the gonadotropins: (1) LOW testosterone → check LH/FSH — HIGH gonadotropins = primary hypogonadism (Klinefelter → karyotype; mumps, trauma, amiodarone), whereas LOW/inappropriately-normal gonadotropins = secondary hypogonadism (Kallmann/IHH, pituitary tumour → MRI). (2) NORMAL testosterone → check prolactin — HIGH prolactin = prolactinoma or drug-induced; NORMAL prolactin = drug-induced AR blockade (spironolactone, cimetidine, 5α-reductase inhibitor, bicalutamide). (3) HIGH testosterone → check LH — HIGH LH = androgen resistance (PAIS/MAIS, celiac-related acquired resistance, 5α-reductase deficiency), while NORMAL LH points to physiologic/idiopathic. Confirm by mechanism: karyotype if primary; pituitary MRI if secondary or high prolactin; AR gene sequencing if high T + high LH; celiac serology if high T + high LH + low DHT; and a drug-withdrawal (dechallenge) trial when a culprit drug is plausible.

```
                  GYNECOMASTIA
                       |
            History + exam + drug review
                       |
    Panel: T, free T, LH, FSH, E2, PRL, TSH, beta-hCG, LFTs
                       |
   +-------------------+--------------------+
   |                   |                    |
 LOW T              NORMAL T              HIGH T
   |                   |                    |
 LH/FSH high        PRL high             LH high
  = PRIMARY        = prolactinoma       = ANDROGEN
  (KS, mumps,        / drug-induced       RESISTANCE
  amiodarone)       |                    (PAIS/MAIS,
   |               PRL normal             celiac, 5a-RD)
 LH/FSH low        = drug AR block         |
  = SECONDARY      (spirono,            LH normal
  (Kallmann,        cimetidine,         = physiologic /
  pituitary)        5a-RI)               idiopathic
```
keyPoints: First fork is testosterone; second fork is LH/FSH (if low T), prolactin (if normal T), or LH (if high T)
An inappropriately low/normal LH with low T = secondary cause → pituitary MRI
High T + high LH = androgen resistance → AR sequencing or celiac serology (if DHT low)
When a drug is plausible, a dechallenge trial is both diagnostic and therapeutic

items.title: The ten cases at a glance
items.content: Bucket A — androgen deficiency. Case 1: Klinefelter (47,XXY) + non-functioning macroadenoma — ↓T, ↑FSH, inappropriately normal LH → T replacement + MRI surveillance. Case 2: Kallmann — ↓T, ↓LH, ↓FSH, anosmia, olfactory-bulb agenesis → T replacement, later hCG/FSH for fertility. Case 3: amiodarone primary hypogonadism — ↓T, ↑↑LH/FSH, testicular atrophy → stop drug + T replacement. Bucket B — hyperprolactinemia. Case 4: macroprolactinoma — PRL ~18,400 mIU/L, ↓T, low-normal LH → cabergoline. Case 5: risperidone hyperprolactinemia — PRL 48 ng/mL, normal T → dose reduction, spontaneous resolution. Bucket C — androgen resistance. Case 6: PAIS (AR p.Thr576Ile) — ↑T, ↑LH, normal DHT → mastectomy + supraphysiologic T. Case 7: celiac acquired resistance — ↑T, ↑LH, ↓DHT → strict gluten-free diet. Bucket D — anti-androgen drugs (all normal serum T). Case 8: spironolactone — AR blockade → switch to eplerenone. Case 9: finasteride — ↓DHT, T shunted to E2 → stop drug (residual fibrosis possible). Case 10: cimetidine — direct AR antagonism → switch to famotidine.
keyPoints: Low T + high gonadotropins → primary (Cases 1, 3)
Low T + low gonadotropins → secondary (Cases 2, 4)
High T + high LH → resistance (Cases 6, 7)
Normal T + culprit drug → Bucket D (Cases 8–10)

items.title: Caveats, doubt-flags and source notes
items.content: Read these limitations before quoting the atlas. (1) Two cases are representative/composite rather than a single indexed vignette: Case 4 (macroprolactinoma) is built around the representative presentation of the Pinzone et al. 1997 cohort, and Case 10 (cimetidine) supplements the 1980 Pediatrics report with the broader literature (Jensen et al. 1983) — treat their exact numbers as illustrative. (2) Case 7's mechanism (acquired androgen resistance in untreated celiac disease) rests on older work (Green 1977; Farthing 1983); the 'autoantibody to AR' and the magnitude of intestinal 5α-reductase contribution remain incompletely settled — stated here as plausible, not proven. (3) Drug-induced gynecomastia incidence figures (e.g. spironolactone 10–52%, finasteride ~0.4–1.0%) vary widely with dose, duration and ascertainment. (4) Cabergoline for antipsychotic hyperprolactinemia is a last-resort, off-label step with a small risk of exacerbating psychosis. (5) All cases are male-pattern adult/adolescent gynecomastia; the diagnostic thresholds quoted use the original papers' assay reference ranges — always interpret against your own laboratory's ranges. Management statements are educational, not prescriptive.
keyPoints: Cases 4 and 10 are representative/supplemented, not single-vignette — numbers are illustrative
Celiac androgen-resistance mechanism is plausible but not fully settled
Drug-induced incidence figures are ascertainment-dependent
Use your own laboratory's reference ranges; statements are educational

items.question: Why does gynecomastia occur in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (e.g. Kallmann) when both testicular testosterone and estradiol are low?
items.answer: Gynecomastia reflects the estrogen-to-androgen ratio at the breast, not the absolute estradiol level. In hypogonadotropic hypogonadism testicular testosterone falls steeply, so even a low absolute estradiol — sustained by peripheral aromatization of adrenal androgens (e.g. androstenedione) in adipose tissue — represents a relatively unopposed estrogenic signal. Crucially, the absence of androgen-mediated suppression of breast glandular tissue during the pubertal window lets the gland proliferate. So the breast 'sees' a high estrogen:androgen ratio despite low estradiol in absolute terms.
keyPoints: It is the ratio, not the absolute E2, that drives gynecomastia
Peripheral aromatization of adrenal androgens sustains estrogen action
Loss of androgen-mediated suppression of breast tissue is permissive

items.question: Why do only ~8% of hyperprolactinemic men have galactorrhea while gynecomastia is far more common?
items.answer: Lactogenesis requires breast tissue primed by estrogen and progesterone; the adult male breast lacks this priming, so prolactin alone rarely produces milk — hence galactorrhea is uncommon (~8%). Gynecomastia is more frequent because prolactin causes it indirectly: it suppresses GnRH and Leydig-cell steroidogenesis, producing an androgen-deficient (secondary hypogonadal) state that raises the estrogen-to-androgen ratio at the breast. So the common manifestation is the consequence of low testosterone, while the rare one (galactorrhea) needs hormonal priming the male breast usually lacks.
keyPoints: Galactorrhea needs estrogen/progesterone priming the male breast lacks
Gynecomastia is an indirect effect of prolactin-induced androgen deficiency
Galactorrhea in a man still warrants prolactinoma exclusion

items.question: How do you distinguish partial androgen insensitivity (PAIS) from 5α-reductase deficiency at the bench?
items.answer: Both present in a 46,XY individual with undervirilization and can show high/normal testosterone with elevated LH, so the discriminator is the testosterone-to-DHT ratio. In PAIS, 5α-reductase works normally, so DHT and the T:DHT ratio are normal; the defect is in the receptor (AR gene sequencing is confirmatory). In 5α-reductase (type 2) deficiency, T→DHT conversion is impaired, so DHT is low and the T:DHT ratio is markedly elevated (>20:1 prepubertal, often >40:1 after hCG/post-puberty); SRD5A2 sequencing confirms it. Note that the acquired androgen resistance of untreated celiac disease also produces a low DHT (a 5α-reductase-deficiency-like picture) but is reversible with a gluten-free diet — so check celiac serology when the pattern is acquired.
keyPoints: T:DHT ratio is the key: normal in PAIS, markedly elevated in 5α-reductase deficiency
Confirm with AR sequencing (PAIS) vs SRD5A2 sequencing (5α-RD)
Untreated celiac disease mimics low DHT but is acquired and reversible

items.question: How do you induce fertility in a man with congenital hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (e.g. Kallmann) who has been on testosterone replacement?
items.answer: Testosterone replacement virilizes but suppresses spermatogenesis, so it must be stopped and gonadotropin (or GnRH) therapy substituted to drive the testis directly. If the pituitary is responsive, pulsatile GnRH can restore physiologic LH/FSH secretion. More commonly, combined gonadotropin therapy is used: hCG (an LH analogue) to stimulate Leydig-cell intratesticular testosterone, with FSH added (hMG or recombinant FSH) to support Sertoli-cell-driven spermatogenesis. Sperm can appear over several months to a couple of years; outcomes are better with larger baseline testicular volume and no prior cryptorchidism. Fertility can be achieved even after years of prior testosterone use.
keyPoints: Stop testosterone — it suppresses spermatogenesis
Pulsatile GnRH (if pituitary responsive) or hCG + FSH to drive the testis
Spermatogenesis takes months to years; larger testes predict better outcomes

items.stem: A 19-year-old man with bilateral gynecomastia is 196 cm tall with small firm testes (<4 mL) and sparse body hair. Testosterone 180 ng/dL (300–1080), LH 28 mIU/mL (0.6–12.1), FSH 42 mIU/mL (1.0–12.0). What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: Tall stature + small firm testes + primary hypogonadism (low T with high LH/FSH) + gynecomastia is classic Klinefelter syndrome; confirm by karyotype. Kallmann causes low LH/FSH; PAIS shows normal/high T with elevated LH; 5α-reductase deficiency shows normal/high T with an elevated T:DHT ratio.

items.stem: A 17-year-old has absent puberty, anosmia and bilateral gynecomastia. LH <0.3 mIU/mL, FSH 0.5 mIU/mL, testosterone 0.42 ng/mL. Which MRI finding is most likely?
items.explanation: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism + anosmia = Kallmann syndrome; the MRI hallmark is bilateral olfactory-bulb agenesis/hypoplasia from failed GnRH-neuron migration. The other findings do not fit the anosmia + low-gonadotropin picture.

items.stem: A 44-year-old man has gynecomastia, galactorrhea and low libido; prolactin is 18,400 mIU/L. Which best explains the mechanism of his gynecomastia?
options: Direct prolactin stimulation of breast glandular tissue
Prolactin-mediated suppression of GnRH causing secondary hypogonadism
Prolactin-induced increase in peripheral aromatase activity
Concomitant TSH elevation stimulating breast tissue
items.explanation: Prolactin suppresses GnRH → low LH/FSH → low testosterone → raised estrogen-to-androgen ratio. Prolactin does not directly stimulate male breast tissue to produce gynecomastia; its effect is indirect via androgen deficiency.

items.stem: A man on risperidone develops gynecomastia and galactorrhea 2 weeks after starting it. Prolactin is 48 ng/mL and testosterone is normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Trial dose reduction or switch to a prolactin-sparing agent (e.g. aripiprazole)
items.explanation: The tight temporal relationship and modest elevation make drug-induced hyperprolactinemia most likely. Management hierarchy: reduce dose → switch to a prolactin-sparing agent → adjunctive aripiprazole → cabergoline as last resort. Abrupt discontinuation risks psychiatric decompensation; MRI/cabergoline are premature.

items.stem: A 16-year-old with gynecomastia, penoscrotal hypospadias and a bifid scrotum has testosterone 985 ng/dL, LH 14.2 mIU/mL, a normal DHT and a 46,XY karyotype. What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: High T + high LH + an undervirilized 46,XY male with a normal DHT (and normal T:DHT ratio) = PAIS; confirm with AR sequencing. 5α-reductase deficiency shows an elevated T:DHT ratio; Klinefelter shows low T with high LH/FSH; a Leydig-cell tumour would show a testicular mass and suppressed LH.

items.stem: A 32-year-old man with celiac disease has gynecomastia despite reported gluten-free adherence. Testosterone 987 ng/dL, LH 18.5 mIU/mL, DHT 18 ng/dL (low). What best explains this pattern?
options: Acquired 5α-reductase impairment from ongoing villous atrophy
items.explanation: High T + high LH + low DHT in active celiac disease reflects impaired intestinal 5α-reductase activity and acquired androgen resistance — a reversible mimic of 5α-reductase deficiency that improves with a strict gluten-free diet. An AR mutation would give a normal/high DHT; the other options do not fit the low-DHT pattern.

items.stem: A hypertensive 52-year-old man on spironolactone develops unilateral painful gynecomastia after 12 months. Testosterone and LH are normal. What is the best management?
options: Add testosterone gel to raise androgen tone
items.explanation: Eplerenone is a selective mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist with negligible androgen-receptor affinity and is the preferred substitute. Tamoxifen treats the symptom without addressing the cause; mastectomy is premature; testosterone is ineffective against receptor blockade.

items.stem: A 21-year-old on finasteride 1 mg/day for alopecia develops gynecomastia. Which mechanism best explains this?
options: Finasteride is a direct estrogen-receptor agonist
Blockade of T→DHT conversion leaves more testosterone available for aromatization to estradiol
Finasteride lowers SHBG, increasing free estradiol
items.explanation: 5α-reductase inhibition prevents T→DHT conversion, lowering potent androgenic tone and shunting testosterone toward aromatization to estradiol — falling androgen-to-estrogen ratio at the breast. Finasteride is not an ER agonist and does not meaningfully alter prolactin or SHBG.

items.stem: Which H₂-receptor antagonist carries the highest risk of gynecomastia through direct androgen-receptor antagonism?
items.explanation: Cimetidine's imidazole ring binds the androgen receptor, giving it clinically significant anti-androgen activity. Famotidine and nizatidine (thiazole ring) and ranitidine have negligible AR affinity.

items.stem: Which biochemical pattern is most consistent with congenital androgen resistance (PAIS)?
options: Low testosterone, high LH, high FSH
High testosterone, high LH, normal FSH
Low testosterone, low LH, low FSH
Normal testosterone, normal LH, high FSH
items.explanation: In PAIS the androgen-resistant hypothalamus/pituitary drive LH up, raising testosterone, while FSH stays normal because Sertoli-cell inhibin-B feedback is androgen-independent and intact. Option A is primary hypogonadism; option C is secondary hypogonadism.

items.statement: In a man with very low testosterone, an inappropriately normal LH reliably excludes a coexisting pituitary lesion.
items.explanation: The opposite is true: in primary hypogonadism LH should be elevated, so a non-elevated (inappropriately normal or low) LH for the degree of hypogonadism should prompt evaluation for a superimposed secondary cause — a pituitary MRI (± prolactin and iron studies). This is exactly the clue that unmasked the macroadenoma in Case 1.

items.statement: Galactorrhea is more common than gynecomastia in hyperprolactinemic men.
items.explanation: Galactorrhea is uncommon in men (~8%) because the male breast lacks the estrogen/progesterone priming needed for lactogenesis. Gynecomastia is far more frequent because prolactin causes it indirectly, through prolactin-induced androgen deficiency.

items.statement: Eplerenone has substantially lower androgen-receptor affinity than spironolactone, making it the preferred mineralocorticoid antagonist when gynecomastia is a concern.
items.explanation: Eplerenone is a selective mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist with roughly 100–1000-fold lower androgen-receptor affinity than spironolactone, so it carries far less risk of gynecomastia and is the preferred substitute (as in Case 8).

items.statement: In partial androgen insensitivity syndrome, FSH is typically elevated to the same degree as LH.
items.explanation: FSH is usually normal in androgen insensitivity even when LH is high, because Sertoli cells lack functional androgen receptors and their inhibin-B feedback (which selectively restrains FSH) remains intact. LH rises because androgen-mediated negative feedback at the hypothalamus/pituitary is impaired.

items.assertion: Drug-induced gynecomastia usually develops despite a normal serum testosterone.
items.reason: Many culprit drugs cause gynecomastia by antagonising the androgen receptor at the breast rather than by lowering circulating testosterone.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. In Cases 8–10 (spironolactone, finasteride, cimetidine) serum testosterone was normal; the lesion is reduced androgen signalling at the breast (AR antagonism, or shunting of T to estradiol), not a low circulating testosterone.

items.assertion: Finasteride-induced gynecomastia always resolves completely once the drug is stopped.
items.reason: Glandular breast tissue can become fibrotic ('burnt-out') and stop responding to subsequent hormonal change.
items.explanation: The assertion is false (resolution is often only partial — Case 9 had residual gynecomastia at 10 months) while the reason is true and is precisely why resolution can be incomplete: long-standing tissue fibroses and becomes hormonally unresponsive, sometimes requiring surgery. A true reason with a false assertion = option 3.

items.assertion: Cimetidine and famotidine carry an equal risk of gynecomastia.
items.reason: Both H₂-receptor antagonists contain an imidazole ring that binds the androgen receptor.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Cimetidine carries a much higher risk because its imidazole ring binds the AR; famotidine contains a thiazole ring with negligible AR affinity, so it is the safer substitute. Both false = option 4.

items.question: A 46,XY man has high testosterone, high LH, normal FSH and a normal T:DHT ratio with undervirilization and gynecomastia. What is the diagnosis and the single confirmatory test?
items.answer: Partial androgen insensitivity syndrome (PAIS); confirm with androgen-receptor (AR) gene sequencing. The normal T:DHT ratio excludes 5α-reductase deficiency, and FSH is normal because Sertoli-cell inhibin-B feedback is intact.

items.question: Name the biochemical triad that flags the acquired androgen resistance of untreated celiac disease, and the one intervention that reverses it.
items.answer: High testosterone + high LH + low DHT (a reversible mimic of 5α-reductase deficiency). The reversing intervention is a strictly enforced gluten-free diet, which heals the mucosa and normalises DHT, LH and gynecomastia over months.

items.question: A hypertensive man on spironolactone develops gynecomastia with normal testosterone and LH. What single medication change is the best management?
items.answer: Switch spironolactone to eplerenone, a selective mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist with negligible androgen-receptor affinity — this is both diagnostic (dechallenge) and therapeutic, and maintains blood-pressure/potassium control.
